title: GitHub Review by berendt
summary: The reviewer flags two blocking issues: (1) the webhook (`keystone_webhook.go`) lacks defense-in-depth validation for the new `networkPolicy` field—specifically, it should reject empty `ingress` when `networkPolicy` is set, matching the checks done for every other optional spec field; (2) there are no Chainsaw E2E tests for the NetworkPolicy feature, breaking the project's pattern of having E2E coverage for every feature under `tests/e2e/keystone/`.
external_feedback:
> Reviewed by planwerk-review d68e5e8 with Claude CLI

<!-- planwerk-review: blocking=2 critical=0 warning=2 info=2 recommendation=CUSTOM -->

## BLOCKING (2)

### B-001: Missing webhook defense-in-depth validation for NetworkPolicy
**File**: `keystone_webhook.go:260` — **Fix**: ASK — **Confidence**: verified — **Pattern**: Fail closed when CRD validation can be bypassed

**Problem**: The webhook validates every other optional spec field with defense-in-depth checks (autoscaling, cache, database, policyOverrides), but the networkPolicy field has no webhook validation. The reconciler has a defensive guard at reconcile_networkpolicy.go:61, but the webhook is the earliest and most appropriate place for admission-time rejection.

**Code**:
```
// reconcile_networkpolicy.go:61
if len(keystone.Spec.NetworkPolicy.Ingress) == 0 {
    return ctrl.Result{}, fmt.Errorf("spec.networkPolicy.ingress must not be empty...")
}
```

**Action Required**: Add webhook validation for networkPolicy after the autoscaling block, checking that ingress is not empty when networkPolicy is set.

**Suggested Fix**:
```
	// Defense-in-depth networkPolicy ingress check alongside the
	// +kubebuilder:validation:XValidation CEL rule on NetworkPolicySpec (CC-0039).
	if k.Spec.NetworkPolicy != nil {
		if len(k.Spec.NetworkPolicy.Ingress) == 0 {
			allErrs = append(allErrs, field.Required(
				specPath.Child("networkPolicy", "ingress"),
				"at least one ingress source must be specified",
			))
		}
	}
```

---

### B-002: Missing Chainsaw E2E tests for NetworkPolicy
**File**: `tests/e2e/keystone/:1` — **Fix**: ASK — **Confidence**: verified — **Pattern**: Test Coverage for New Code

**Problem**: The project has Chainsaw E2E tests for every existing feature under tests/e2e/keystone/ (autoscaling, basic-deployment, brownfield-database, credential-rotation, deletion-cleanup, fernet-rotation, image-upgrade, invalid-cr, middleware-config, missing-secret, policy-overrides, scale). No network-policy E2E test directory was added in this PR.

**Code**:
```
tests/e2e/keystone/autoscaling/
tests/e2e/keystone/basic-deployment/
tests/e2e/keystone/brownfield-database/
... (no network-policy/ directory)
```

**Action Required**: Add tests/e2e/keystone/network-policy/chainsaw-test.yaml with test steps covering: (1) applying a Keystone CR with networkPolicy set and verifying the NetworkPolicy object exists, (2) removing networkPolicy and verifying the NetworkPolicy is deleted, (3) updating ingress sources and verifying the NetworkPolicy is updated. Follow the pattern in tests/e2e/keystone/autoscaling/.

**Suggested Fix**:
```
Add tests/e2e/keystone/network-policy/chainsaw-test.yaml with test steps following the pattern in tests/e2e/keystone/autoscaling/, covering create, update, and delete lifecycle paths.
```

---

## WARNING (2)

### W-001: NetworkPolicy reconcile ordering may be too late in the chain
**File**: `keystone_controller.go:103-113` — **Fix**: ASK — **Confidence**: likely — **Pattern**: Validate reconcile step ordering matches data dependencies

**Problem**: reconcileNetworkPolicy runs after reconcileDeployment and reconcileHPA. On a fresh deployment, Keystone pods will be running and accepting unrestricted traffic before the NetworkPolicy is applied. For a security-hardening feature, pods should be born into a restricted network.

**Code**:
```
if result, err := r.reconcileDeployment(ctx, &keystone, configMapName); !result.IsZero() || err != nil {
    return r.updateStatus(ctx, &keystone, result, err)
}
if result, err := r.reconcileHPA(ctx, &keystone); !result.IsZero() || err != nil {
    return r.updateStatus(ctx, &keystone, result, err)
}
if result, err := r.reconcileNetworkPolicy(ctx, &keystone); !result.IsZero() || err != nil {
    return r.updateStatus(ctx, &keystone, result, err)
}
```

**Action Required**: Consider moving reconcileNetworkPolicy before reconcileDeployment. The NetworkPolicy has no data dependency on the Deployment — it uses selectorLabels derived from the CR, not from the Deployment.

**Suggested Fix**:
```
Move the reconcileNetworkPolicy call block before the reconcileDeployment call block so that the NetworkPolicy is created before any pods are started.
```

**Related**: W-002

---

### W-002: Controller test assertions claim NetworkPolicy doesn't run when deployment is not ready, but reconcile ordering contradicts this
**File**: `keystone_controller_test.go:584-585` — **Fix**: ASK — **Confidence**: verified

**Problem**: The test TestReconcile_ReadyFalseWhenDeploymentNotAvailable asserts that NetworkPolicyReady is nil when deployment is not available. This is only correct because NetworkPolicy runs after Deployment in the current ordering. The NetworkPolicy has no actual dependency on Deployment readiness, so this test is testing ordering rather than semantic correctness. If ordering changes, this test breaks.

**Code**:
```
g.Expect(meta.FindStatusCondition(updated.Status.Conditions, "NetworkPolicyReady")).To(BeNil(),
    "NetworkPolicyReady should not be set when deployment is not available")
```

**Action Required**: If reconcile ordering is changed to apply NetworkPolicy before Deployment, update this test assertion to expect NetworkPolicyReady to be set even when the Deployment is not available.

**Suggested Fix**:
```
Update the test to reflect the new ordering: if NetworkPolicy runs before Deployment, NetworkPolicyReady should be set regardless of Deployment availability.
```

**Related**: W-001

---

## INFO (2)

### I-001: Egress rules allow any destination on MariaDB/Memcached ports
**File**: `reconcile_networkpolicy.go:167-173` — **Fix**: ASK — **Confidence**: verified

**Problem**: The auto-derived MariaDB and Memcached egress rules restrict only the port, not the destination. A NetworkPolicyEgressRule with Ports but no To allows traffic to any destination on that port.

**Code**:
```
if keystone.Spec.Database.ClusterRef != nil {
    port3306 := intstr.FromInt32(3306)
    rules = append(rules, networkingv1.NetworkPolicyEgressRule{
        Ports: []networkingv1.NetworkPolicyPort{
            {Protocol: &tcp, Port: &port3306},
        },
    })
}
```

**Action Required**: Document this as a known limitation for future tightening. Tightening requires resolving the actual service pod labels from the ClusterRef, which adds complexity and a potential circular dependency.

**Suggested Fix**:
```
Add a code comment or design doc entry documenting that egress rules currently allow any destination on the specified ports, and that future iterations should resolve actual service pod labels from ClusterRef to restrict destinations.
```

---

### I-002: npNormalizeMap duplicates normalizeMap in common package
**File**: `reconcile_networkpolicy.go:276-281` — **Fix**: AUTO-FIX — **Confidence**: verified

**Problem**: npNormalizeMap is functionally identical to normalizeMap in internal/common/deployment/deployment.go. The code comment acknowledges this is intentional, waiting for a second consumer to justify extraction.

**Code**:
```
func npNormalizeMap(...) { ... }
```

**Action Required**: No action needed now. When a second consumer appears, extract to a shared utility. The current approach is acceptable per the project's own conventions.

**Suggested Fix**:
```
No change required. The duplication is intentional and documented. Extract to a shared utility when a second consumer appears.
```

---

## Summary

This is a well-structured PR that follows established sub-reconciler patterns closely. Code quality is high with proper error wrapping, defensive guards, snapshot-based no-op detection, and comprehensive unit tests covering all lifecycle paths plus error injection. The two blocking findings are missing webhook defense-in-depth validation (which every other optional field has) and missing Chainsaw E2E tests (which every other feature has). The reconcile ordering discussion is worth resolving before merge.

| Severity | Count |
|----------|-------|
| BLOCKING | 2 |
| CRITICAL | 0 |
| WARNING  | 2 |
| INFO     | 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation**: Do not merge until the two BLOCKING findings are resolved: add webhook validation for networkPolicy ingress and add Chainsaw E2E tests. The WARNING about reconcile ordering should be discussed and decided on before merge, but is not strictly blocking.
resolution_summary: Both blocking review items were addressed. B-001 (missing webhook validation) was resolved by adding a defense-in-depth check in keystone_webhook.go that rejects networkPolicy with empty ingress at admission time, matching the suggested fix exactly, with three corresponding unit tests added to keystone_webhook_test.go (nil-valid, with-ingress-valid, empty-ingress-rejected) plus updates to the existing aggregate-validation test. B-002 (missing Chainsaw E2E tests) was resolved by creating a full test suite under tests/e2e/keystone/network-policy/ with four files (chainsaw-test.yaml, 00-keystone-cr.yaml, 01-patch-update-ingress.yaml, 02-patch-disable-networkpolicy.yaml) covering create, update, and delete flows. Additionally, changes were made to files not mentioned in the review: (1) keystone_controller.go — reconcile ordering was changed to run NetworkPolicy before Deployment so pods are born into a restricted network, (2) keystone_controller_test.go — the existing TestReconcile_ReadyFalseWhenDeploymentNotAvailable test was updated to reflect the new ordering where NetworkPolicyReady is set even when deployment is unavailable, and (3) reconcile_networkpolicy.go — NOTE comments were added to MariaDB and Memcached egress rules documenting that they restrict only ports (not destinations) as a known limitation deferred to a future iteration.
